subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Raw and processed data presented in clear, precise tables and graphs
Thermal time constants calculated with correct uncertainty propagation formula
Data communication (tables, legends, models) is generally clear and precise
weaknesses: Omitted uncertainties for temperature readings and regression parameters; no error bars on graphs
Inconsistencies in unit notation and minor typographical errors in logarithmic plots
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data processing and presentation are strong (5â6 band) but evidence of uncertainties is incomplete and there are some significant omissions and inaccuracies (3â4 band). Overall the work sits firmly in the middle band, meriting 4 marks.

strengths: Focused research question placed in a specific baking-to-laboratory context
Clear theoretical framing using Fourierâs law and specific heat capacity
Detailed materials list and systematic procedure supporting reproducibility
weaknesses: Omitted exact cuboid fabrication dimensions and ambient conditions
Some assumptions (e.g. perfect insulation) not explicitly stated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs research design demonstrates a specific and appropriate context with a wellâjustified research question and strong theoretical underpinning (band 5â6). The methodology is reproducible with only minor ambiguities (band 3â4), and methodological considerations are described rather than fully explained. On balance this criterion aligns with an upperâband 5.

strengths: Conclusion directly addresses the research question and quantitatively references Ï values
Justified through relevant comparison to Fourierâs law and accepted physics
Consistent and fully supported by the analysis presented
weaknesses: Comparison to scientific context is correct but lacks quantitative crossâcheck or literature values
Brief discussion of followâup measurements could enrich depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is fully consistent with the analysis and clearly justified (5â6 band). The comparison to accepted scientific context is relevant but not deeply quantitative, placing it at the top of the band without reaching exemplary depth.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses linked to environmental variation and template accuracy
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements (additional probes, more container mockâups)
weaknesses: Does not explain the relative impact of each limitation on results
Suggested improvements lack quantitative justification of their effectiveness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation describes specific weaknesses and offers realistic improvements (band 3â4) but stops short of explaining their relative impacts or fully justifying how improvements reduce uncertainties. This places the rubric performance at the lower end of the band, warranting 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states a focused research question â âhow inner surface area affects the rate of cooling of a fixed volume of waterâ â and anchors it in a real-world baking scenario, then narrows immediately to a controlled laboratory set-up.  Background on conduction, Fourierâs law and specific heat capacity provides a clear scientific frame that is directly relevant to the investigation.  The context is therefore both specific and appropriate, though not exceptionally novel, warranting placement in the upper band rather than full marks.

Uncertainties are considered for area, mass, thickness and propagated to the thermal time constant; percentage uncertainty is discussed.  However, uncertainty of temperature readings and of regression parameters is omitted, and error bars are not shown on graphs, representing significant gaps.  Thus the work exceeds âlimited evidenceâ but contains notable omissions, aligning with the 3-4 band.

Relevant methodological considerations â constant water mass, foil thickness, starting temperature, insulated box, multiple trials â are each described and linked to the research question.  The student justifies why convection and radiation are minimised and gives a worked uncertainty propagation.  These points show clear understanding, but explanations of how each factor quantitatively influences data sufficiency remain partial, so âdescribedâ (not fully âexplainedâ) is the best match.

Two concrete weaknesses are identified (day-to-day environmental variation and template accuracy), each described in context.  The student indicates how these could have affected temperature readings and surface-area precision, but stops short of analysing the relative magnitude of each effect on final results.  Thus the strand meets âdescribes specific weaknessesâ in the 3-4 band.

Procedures list equipment, volumes, temperatures, intervals, number of trials, and show exemplar data-logger output, allowing a competent peer to repeat the work with only minor questions (exact cuboid dimensions and ambient conditions are not fully specified).  The description is generally reproducible but still contains a few ambiguities, especially about foil fabrication and day-to-day environment, so the strand sits comfortably at the top of the 3-4 band.

Data are averaged, linearised, and gradients extracted; thermal time constants are calculated with propagated uncertainty.  Calculations are mostly sound, yet a few inconsistencies appear (Kelvin treated like Â°C in logarithmic plot, small typographical error in Ï equation).  These amount to âsome significant omissions or inaccuraciesâ rather than âmajorâ, so the middle band and upper score are appropriate.

For every limitation a realistic, relevant improvement is proposed (parallel runs with extra box/probe; increase number of container mock-ups).  These improvements are described with sufficient detail to be actionable, though their prospective impact is not fully explained, keeping the strand at the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Raw and processed data are organised into comprehensive tables with units and uncertainties; graphs are labelled, include legends and fitted models; sample calculations are shown in appendices.  Presentation is both clear (easy to read) and precise (correct units, significant figures generally respected).  Minor formatting issues do not detract, so performance sits firmly in the 5â6 band, albeit not outstanding enough for 6 marks.

The student links findings to Fourierâs law and theoretical inverse proportionality between Ï and area, demonstrating relevant comparison with accepted physics.  The discussion is correct but brief and largely qualitative; no literature values or detailed quantitative cross-checks are provided.  Hence the work is âsome relevant comparisonâ rather than fully justified, meriting 3-4 band.

The conclusion directly addresses the research question, quantitatively referencing figure trends and Ï values, and is consistent with the analysis presented.  While the student concedes data are insufficient to extend to room-temperature equilibrium, the main claim remains fully supported; the reasoning is clear and justified, meeting the descriptors for the highest band short of exemplary depth.

annotations.comment: The methodology description omits key details on cuboid fabrication and ambient conditions; specify foil template dimensions and record room temperature/humidity.
In the derivation, explicitly state boundary assumptions (e.g. perfect insulation, negligible convection) before applying Fourierâs law to clarify model validity.
The research question is grounded in a specific baking-to-laboratory context and framed by relevant physics, demonstrating a clear, appropriate justification for the study.
Consider elaborating how conduction quantitatively dominates convective and radiative modes in this closed system, perhaps by comparing magnitudes from Fourierâs law.
The background footnote relies on a general web overview; consider adding a peer-reviewed source to strengthen the scientific foundation.
The derivation to $$T_{water}(t)=(T_i-T_r)e^{-\frac{kA}{mcx}t}+T_r$$ is clear and logically structured, aiding comprehension of the model.
Clarify the transition from finite differences (Î) to differentials (d); specify limit assumptions when moving to $$\frac{dT}{dt}$$ notation.
Figure 2 clearly illustrates the experimental setup with appropriate labels and scale, enhancing reproducibility and clarity.
Units for the independent variable appear inconsistently formatted as cmÂ²; ensure uniform notation (e.g. cmÂ²) throughout variables section.
The controlled variables table systematically links each factor to measurement and significance, demonstrating strong methodological control.
Ambient room temperature is not listed among controlled variables; include this to ensure consistency across trials.
The materials list covers all apparatus and instrumentation, providing a solid basis for reproducibility.
Ethical/environmental considerations discuss waste reduction, but could address disposal of used foil and water return to drains.
Raw data tables include time and temperature with uncertainties and consistent significant figures, enhancing transparency.
Quantify the relative impact of day-to-day environmental variations by measuring ambient temperature differences between trials.
Procedure steps are detailed and systematic, supporting reproducibility and consistency across trials.
Dimensions of cuboids are referenced elsewhere but not in the procedure; include a direct table of length/width/height here for clarity.
Table column headings are truncated (âTimâ, âTempeâ); correct labels (e.g. Time (min), Temperature (K)) to avoid ambiguity.
The formula for absolute uncertainty $$\Delta \tau=(\frac{\Delta m}{m}+\frac{\Delta x}{x}+\frac{\Delta A}{A})\tau$$ is correctly applied and documented.
Uncertainties for regression parameters and measurement of temperature readings are omitted; include these and add error bars to graphs.
The comprehensive table of averaged temperatures is clear, but summarizing key trends graphically would improve readability.
Highlight anomalies (e.g. Aâ/Aâ) in the data table before discussion to guide readers to key deviations.
Figure 4 is well-executed but lacks error bars to reflect measurement uncertainties; include these for precision.
Introduce the regression equation for the linearized plot (Figure 5) in the text to quantify trend parameters.
The discussion compellingly links curve gradients to surface area, demonstrating clear understanding of rate-of-cooling behavior.
Could the deviations in Aâ/Aâ gradients be further tested by rerunning those specific containers under controlled conditions?
A brief mention of potential follow-up measurements to approach room-temperature equilibrium would enrich the conclusion.
Thermal time constants are tabulated clearly, quantitatively supporting the inverse relationship with surface area.
Scientific notation in Table 3 uses âÃ 10-3â without superscript; ensure exponents are formatted (e.g. Ã 10â»Â³).
The conclusion correctly summarizes findings and aligns with analysis, justifying the proportional relationship between area and cooling rate.
Strengths in evaluation effectively link percentage uncertainties, trial number, and initial conditions to reliability.
The extension section proposes diverse, feasible follow-up experiments using alternative materials and heat-transfer modes.
Suggested improvements are realistic but lack explanation of how they quantitatively reduce specific uncertainties.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
